turns.content: Let's get straight to the V2 timeline. The V2 Planning document sets a General Availability for Q1 2027. Based on my review of the technical debt and roadmap, this seems incredibly ambitious. What's the exact target date for GA?

According to the "Timeline & Milestones" section of the V2 Planning document (Section 8, Page 11), the target for V2 General Availability is January 2027. This follows the V2 Release Candidate in December 2026.

January 2027. That's aggressive. The V2 Planning doc itself calls out significant tech debt, like the monolithic configuration database. The Operations doc also flags sprint velocity as 'At Risk'. How can the timeline be realistic when we're already missing velocity targets and have a major database decomposition to do?

You are correct. Section 2.3 of the V2 Planning document explicitly identifies the monolithic PostgreSQL cluster as a scaling bottleneck to be addressed in V2. Additionally, the Operational KPIs table in the Strategy & Operations document (Section 4) shows current sprint velocity at 208 story points against a target of 220, labeling it "At Risk". The plan seems to account for this, as the Engineering Roadmap in the Technical Architecture document (Section 8.1) allocates 20% of each sprint to technical debt reduction.

That 20% allocation is already being exceeded, according to the ops doc. Another thing that doesn't add up is the customer feedback on observability. The internal observability stack described in the Technical Architecture document seems mature—Prometheus, ELK, OpenTelemetry. It should be more than enough for enterprise SLAs. So why are customers so dissatisfied with the Insight module?

That's a key distinction in the documents. The observability stack detailed in the Technical Architecture document (Section 6) is for internal operational monitoring. Customer feedback, however, relates to the customer-facing `Ficduct Insight` module. The Customer Feedback report (Section 3) gives Insight the lowest satisfaction score of any product area at 3.6 out of 5, with customers citing its limitations compared to dedicated BI tools.

I see. So our internal visibility is strong, but we're not exposing that capability to customers. It's the 'black box' problem I noted last time. This means the Insight module needs a complete overhaul, not just a few tweaks. That's another major dependency for the V2 timeline.

Correct. The V2 Planning document corroborates this. The "Insight Module Overhaul" is a core part of the plan, as described in Section 4.2. The planned improvements include a new dashboard builder, over 10 new chart types, drill-down capabilities, and natural language querying to address the gaps identified in customer feedback.
A full BI-lite module, plus a database decomposition, plus an active-active migration. This brings me to hiring. What are the specific headcount growth targets for engineering to support this work?

There are two relevant figures. The Strategy & Operations document (Section 2.2) outlines a company-wide growth plan from 120 to 170 FTEs, with +24 hires in engineering. More specifically, the Technical Architecture document (Section 8.2) states the engineering organization plans to grow from 41 to 65 engineers by Q4 2026.

Growing from 41 to 65 is a 58% increase in the engineering team in under a year. The Technical Architecture doc also specifies hiring for senior Rust and ML engineers, which are not easy roles to fill. The hiring bottleneck is already flagged as a risk. This seems like a major threat to the timeline.

Yes, the hiring priorities for senior Rust engineers and ML engineers are listed in Section 8.2 of the Technical Architecture document. The Risk Management Framework in the Strategy & Operations document (Section 7) also identifies "Hiring bottleneck" as a risk with a score of 9, the joint-highest on the register. Mitigations include using external recruiting agencies and increasing referral bonuses.

So to summarize from an engineering standpoint: we have major tech debt to resolve, a full BI module to build, an aggressive and risky hiring plan for specialized roles, and we're already missing sprint velocity targets. The Q1 2027 GA date for V2 is not just ambitious, it's at high risk of delay.

Your summary reflects the risks outlined in the technical and operational documents. From the product perspective, the V2 Planning document frames the timeline as achievable. Section 8.2, "Success Criteria," is built around this date, targeting 50+ new logo acquisitions attributable to V2 within six months of GA. Furthermore, the detailed migration path in Section 7.2 suggests a high degree of confidence from the planning team.

Confidence doesn't ship code. This disconnect is a problem. It's the same pattern I see with marketing. Pull up the claims around 'AI-Assisted Mapping'. I recall seeing it positioned as a current capability.

The documents present conflicting information on that feature. The Product Overview (Section 10) lists "AI-assisted mapping" as a current competitive strength. The Customer Feedback report (Section 5) even includes a testimonial from an Integration Architect praising it: "The AI-assisted mapping saved our team at least 40 hours... It correctly mapped 85% of fields on the first try." However, the Engineering Roadmap (Section 8) and the V2 Planning document (Section 5.1 and 9) clearly identify the "AI Schema Mapper" as a new V2 feature targeted for beta in Q3 2026.

Exactly. A customer is quoted praising a feature that isn't even in beta yet. This is a serious credibility issue. Marketing is selling the roadmap, not the product. This makes my point about the entire V2 plan: the on-paper schedule and the reality of the engineering work required are two different things.

To be precise, the testimonial from the Integration Architect at Meridian Health is in Section 5 of the Customer Feedback document. The V2 timeline in Section 8 of the V2 Planning document schedules the "AI Schema Mapper Beta" for July 2026, with the feature itself being a P0 priority for V2.

This is a recurring theme. Given the scope of V2—the overhaul of Insight, the active-active migration, the new AI service, and the tech debt—what percentage of engineering capacity is actually allocated to the V2 program?

According to the "Resource Allocation" section of the V2 Planning document (Section 8.1), the V2 program will consume approximately 65% of engineering capacity from Q2 2026 through Q1 2027. The remaining 35% is allocated to V1 maintenance, bug fixes, and incremental improvements.
